ui.heroBody: This track now starts from Go basics and builds toward interfaces, error handling, concurrency, the runtime, escape analysis, and garbage collection. The goal is to understand not only how to write Go, but why the language and runtime behave that way.

01_go_main.title: Package Main and Program Entry
01_go_main.description:
A runnable Go program needs `package main` and a `main()` function.

```go
package main

func main() {
    fmt.Println("boot")
}
```

That tells the toolchain where executable control begins.

q2.question: Why must an executable use `package main`?
options: The Go tool looks for a `main` package with a `main` function as the entry point.
Only `package main` can import the standard library.
It forces every variable to be global.
explanations: Correct. That is how the Go toolchain identifies an executable program.
Any package can import the standard library.
Package choice does not make variables global.
q2.answerReason: A runnable Go binary needs one package and one function that the toolchain recognizes as the entry.

q3.question: What happens if a package is not named `main`?
options: It cannot contain any functions.
It automatically becomes test-only.
It can still be compiled as a library package, but not as the executable entry package.
explanations: Non-main packages can contain plenty of functions.
Package name alone does not make it test-only.
Correct. Non-main packages are reusable packages rather than the binary entry package.
q3.answerReason: Go separates executable entry packages from ordinary libraries through `package main`.

02_variables_zero_values.title: Variables and Zero Values
02_variables_zero_values.description:
Go gives variables a defined zero value even before you assign something meaningful.

```go
var count int
var ok bool
```

This reduces uninitialized-memory style bugs and shapes many Go APIs.

q2.question: What is the zero value of an `int` in Go?
options: `nil`
an undefined value
explanations: `nil` is for certain reference-like types, not `int`.
Correct. Numeric types have numeric zero values.
Go avoids the “undefined local variable contents” model here.
q2.answerReason: Go initializes variables to a defined zero value automatically.

q3.question: Why are zero values important in Go style?
options: They make many types usable immediately without an explicit constructor.
They force every API to use global variables.
They eliminate the need for the type system.
explanations: Correct. Many Go types are designed so the zero value is meaningful.
Zero values do not imply global state.
They complement the type system; they do not replace it.
q3.answerReason: Go APIs often aim for “the zero value is useful,” which keeps simple cases lightweight.

03_functions_returns.title: Functions and Multiple Returns
03_functions_returns.description:
Go functions can return more than one value.

```go
func divide(a, b int) (int, int)
```

This is especially useful for returning a result together with an error or status flag.

q2.question: Why are multiple return values useful in Go?
options: They let one function define multiple packages.
They eliminate the need for any control flow.
They let a function return both a result and extra status such as an error.
explanations: Packages are unrelated.
Control flow is still needed normally.
Correct. This is a major reason Go error handling looks the way it does.
q2.answerReason: Multiple returns make patterns like `(value, ok)` and `(value, err)` natural.

q3.question: What does `left, right := swap(3, 7)` do?
options: It declares only `left` and assigns `right` later.
It declares two variables and binds each returned value positionally.
It copies the function body into `main`.
explanations: Both variables are declared together here.
Correct. Return positions map to assignment positions.
Function calls do not inline by source substitution like that.
q3.answerReason: Go binds multiple return values by position, not by field names.

04_pointers.title: Pointers and Direct Mutation
04_pointers.description:
Go has pointers, but not pointer arithmetic in the C sense.

```go
func inc(n *int) {
    *n++
}
```

Pointers let a function mutate the caller-owned value directly.

q2.question: Why pass `*int` here instead of `int`?
options: The function needs access to the caller-owned value so it can mutate it.
Go forbids passing integers by value.
Only pointer parameters can be printed.
explanations: Correct. A pointer lets the function change the original variable.
Go absolutely allows ordinary value parameters.
Printing is unrelated to pointer-ness.
q2.answerReason: Passing a pointer changes which value is being updated: the caller’s rather than a copy.

q3.question: What is a key constraint on pointers in Go compared with C?
options: Pointers cannot point to values.
Pointers always live on the stack.
Go does not expose arbitrary pointer arithmetic in safe code.
explanations: They can point to values normally.
Escape analysis may move pointed-to values; stack only is false.
Correct. Go keeps pointer use narrower and safer than C.
q3.answerReason: Go uses pointers for sharing access, not as a general low-level arithmetic tool in safe code.

05_arrays_slices.title: Arrays, Slices, and Backing Storage
05_arrays_slices.description:
Arrays have fixed length. Slices are lightweight views over array storage.

```go
nums := []int{1, 2, 3}
part := nums[:2]
```

Understanding that view model is essential for reasoning about mutation and append behavior.

q2.question: Why does changing `part[0]` affect `nums[0]`?
options: Slices synchronize indexes automatically.
The slice still points to the same backing array.
Slicing deep-copies the entire array.
explanations: There is no automatic index synchronization feature.
Correct. A slice is metadata over shared storage.
Basic slicing does not deep-copy the underlying array.
q2.answerReason: The aliasing comes from shared storage, not from a magical “slice sync” rule.

q3.question: What is the main conceptual difference between an array and a slice?
options: An array owns fixed-size storage; a slice describes a region of underlying storage.
An array can hold only numbers, while a slice can hold any type.
A slice is always immutable.
explanations: Correct. Arrays are concrete fixed-size values; slices are flexible views.
Both can be defined over many element types.
Slices are mutable when the underlying elements are mutable.
q3.answerReason: Thinking “slice = descriptor” explains why length, capacity, aliasing, and append matter.

06_maps.title: Map Lookup and Presence
06_maps.description:
Map lookup often returns a value together with a boolean presence flag.

```go
v, ok := m["key"]
```

That distinction matters because a missing key and a stored zero value are different states.

q2.question: Why is the `ok` result useful?
options: It distinguishes a missing key from a present key whose value is the zero value.
It skips hashing for faster lookups.
It converts the map into an ordered structure.
explanations: Correct. Presence and value are separate facts.
Hashing still happens.
Maps remain unordered.
q2.answerReason: Without `ok`, `0` could mean “absent” or “present with value zero.”

q3.question: Why can the value result alone be ambiguous?
options: Because Go randomizes missing results.
Because the zero value might also be a legitimate stored value.
Because maps always return strings internally.
explanations: Go does not randomize the zero value.
Correct. Many value types have meaningful zero values.
Map results keep their declared type.
q3.answerReason: The extra boolean exists because zero and absent are not the same thing.

07_structs_methods.title: Structs and Method Receivers

q2.question: Why is the receiver a pointer here?
options: The method needs to mutate the original struct value.
Go requires all receivers to be pointers.
Only pointer receivers can call `fmt.Println`.
explanations: Correct. Mutation should affect the caller-owned value.
Value receivers also exist.
Printing has nothing to do with receiver kind.
q2.answerReason: Receiver choice decides whether a method sees the same value or a copy.

q3.question: What would likely happen if `Inc` used a value receiver instead?
options: The program would stop compiling.
The call would update both the original and an automatic backup.
The method would increment a copy, leaving the caller-owned struct unchanged.
explanations: Value receivers are legal in Go.
There is no hidden backup update rule.
Correct. Value receivers work on a copy.
q3.answerReason: Methods on value receivers can read well, but mutation of the caller-owned value needs a pointer receiver.

08_interfaces.title: Interfaces and Implicit Satisfaction
08_interfaces.description:
A Go interface describes behavior, and types satisfy it implicitly by method set.

```go
type Runner interface {
    Run() string
}
```

There is no `implements` keyword.

q2.question: How does `Job` satisfy `Runner`?
options: It must declare `implements Runner`.
It has the required method, so it satisfies the interface implicitly.
Interfaces are checked only through reflection at runtime.
explanations: Go has no `implements` keyword.
Correct. Satisfaction is structural and compile-time checked.
The type checker handles this before runtime.
q2.answerReason: Go interfaces are about behavior a value has, not a declared inheritance relationship.

q3.question: Why is implicit satisfaction useful in Go design?
options: Consumers can define small interfaces around the behavior they need without modifying the concrete type.
It forces all packages to depend on one global interface registry.
It prevents compile-time verification of behavior.
explanations: Correct. This supports decoupled, consumer-owned abstractions.
Go’s interface model reduces that kind of central coupling.
Compile-time verification still happens.
q3.answerReason: Small interfaces work well because the consumer can describe behavior locally without demanding upstream type declarations.

09_errors.title: Errors Are Values
09_errors.description:
Go treats errors as ordinary returned values rather than exceptions for common failure paths.

```go
value, err := work()
if err != nil {
    return err
}
```

This keeps failure handling explicit in control flow.

q2.question: Why does Go often return `error` explicitly?
options: To avoid any need for control flow.
Because Go has no function returns.
Because failure is treated as data the caller must inspect explicitly.
explanations: Control flow is still necessary.
Go obviously has function returns.
Correct. The caller decides how to handle the failure path.
q2.answerReason: Go prefers explicit error handling over hidden exception-like jumps for ordinary failures.

q3.question: What does the phrase “errors are values” emphasize?
options: Errors should always be ignored after logging.
An error participates in normal program logic like any other returned value.
Only numeric types can represent errors.
explanations: Ignoring errors is the opposite of the intended lesson.
Correct. Errors are passed, checked, wrapped, and returned explicitly.
Errors are interface values, not numeric-only constructs.
q3.answerReason: The design encourages visible, deliberate failure handling rather than implicit control transfer.

10_defer_panic_recover.title: Defer, Panic, and Recover
10_defer_panic_recover.description:
`defer` schedules cleanup for the surrounding function. `panic` is for unrecoverable states, and `recover` can intercept a panic inside deferred code.

```go
defer file.Close()
```

These tools are powerful, but they are not the normal substitute for explicit `error` returns.

q2.question: What is the main use of `defer`?
options: To ensure cleanup-like work runs when the current function returns.
To launch goroutines automatically.
To convert every error into a panic.
explanations: Correct. `defer` is commonly used for unlock/close/cleanup patterns.
`defer` does not create goroutines.
Errors and panics are different mechanisms.
q2.answerReason: `defer` keeps cleanup near acquisition while preserving correct return-order behavior.

q3.question: Why is `panic` usually not the default error-handling mechanism in Go?
options: Because `panic` is faster than returning errors.
Because `panic` is required for all I/O failures.
Because ordinary failures are usually expected control-flow cases that callers should handle explicitly.
explanations: Speed is not the reason to avoid it.
Ordinary I/O errors are usually returned, not panicked.
Correct. `panic` is for truly exceptional states, not routine failure paths.
q3.answerReason: Using `panic` for common errors makes control flow harder to reason about and recover from cleanly.

11_goroutines.title: Goroutines and Process Lifetime
11_goroutines.description:
A goroutine is a lightweight concurrent execution managed by the Go runtime.

```go
go work()
```

Starting one does not keep the whole process alive by itself.

q1.question: What is printed with the wait group in place?

q2.question: Why is `WaitGroup` used here?
options: To keep `main` alive until the goroutine finishes.
To pin the goroutine to one CPU core.
To share locals by reference automatically.
explanations: Correct. Without synchronization, the program could exit too early.
`WaitGroup` does not do CPU pinning.
Variable capture is unrelated.
q2.answerReason: Concurrency starts work; synchronization determines whether the program waits for it.

q3.question: What is the risk if `main` returns immediately after launching a goroutine?
options: The goroutine becomes a saved daemon.
The process may exit before the goroutine finishes.
The runtime converts the goroutine into a channel.
explanations: Goroutines do not survive process exit.
Correct. Program lifetime still matters.
There is no such conversion.
q3.answerReason: A goroutine is lightweight, but it still lives inside one process that may end first.

12_channels_select.title: Channels and Select
12_channels_select.description:
Channels coordinate communication between goroutines. `select` waits on multiple communication cases.

```go
select {
case v := <-ch:
    fmt.Println(v)
default:
}
```

q2.question: What is the main role of a channel?
options: To sort messages before receipt.
To guarantee all goroutines run sequentially.
To coordinate communication and handoff between goroutines.
explanations: Channels do not sort values.
They can synchronize specific interactions, not make the whole program sequential.
Correct. Communication is the main abstraction.
q2.answerReason: Channels make ownership transfer and coordination explicit.

q3.question: Why is `select` useful?
options: It lets one goroutine react to whichever channel operation becomes ready first.
It replaces all loops in concurrent code.
It forces every case to run in order.
explanations: Correct. `select` multiplexes communication cases.
Loops are still useful.
Ready case selection is not fixed sequential execution.
q3.answerReason: `select` is the communication-oriented control flow primitive for waiting on multiple possible events.

13_mutex_waitgroup.title: Mutexes and Shared State
13_mutex_waitgroup.description:
Channels are not the only concurrency tool. Sometimes you have shared mutable state and need explicit locking.

```go
mu.Lock()
count++
mu.Unlock()
```

A mutex protects the critical section, while a wait group can coordinate completion.

q2.question: What problem does a mutex solve?
options: It makes every goroutine faster automatically.
It prevents conflicting concurrent access to shared mutable state.
It creates new goroutines on lock acquisition.
explanations: A mutex is about correctness, not automatic speedups.
Correct. Mutual exclusion protects critical sections.
Locks do not spawn goroutines.
q2.answerReason: A mutex serializes access where simultaneous mutation would be unsafe.

q3.question: What is the tradeoff when you add a mutex?
options: You eliminate the need to reason about concurrency entirely.
You guarantee maximum parallelism.
You gain correctness for shared updates, but the critical section may reduce concurrency.
explanations: You still need to reason about what is shared and why.
Serialization can reduce, not maximize, parallelism.
Correct. Locks trade some concurrency freedom for safety.
q3.answerReason: Locking is a correctness tool first; performance consequences depend on contention and design.

14_context_timeout.title: Context Cancellation and Deadlines
14_context_timeout.description:
A `context.Context` carries cancellation signals, deadlines, and a small amount of request-scoped metadata through call chains.

```go
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(parent, time.Second)
defer cancel()
```

q2.question: Why pass `context.Context` through a request path?
options: It lets downstream work observe cancellation and deadlines consistently.
It replaces ordinary function parameters for business data.
It forces synchronous execution.
explanations: Correct. It propagates lifecycle signals across layers.
Normal domain inputs are usually clearer as explicit parameters.
Context does not force synchronous behavior.
q2.answerReason: Cancellation only helps when all relevant layers can see the same signal.

q3.question: What kind of data should usually not be hidden in `context.Context`?
options: Cancellation and deadline information.
Ordinary business parameters that should be explicit function arguments.
Request-scoped metadata for cross-cutting middleware concerns.
explanations: Those are core context responsibilities.
Correct. Normal inputs should remain visible in the function signature.
Some scoped metadata can live in context carefully.
q3.answerReason: Context is for lifecycle and cross-cutting signals, not for hiding routine domain data.

15_escape_analysis.title: Escape Analysis and Allocation Placement
15_escape_analysis.description:
Go decides whether a value can stay on the stack or must move to the heap.

```go
func makePtr() *int {
    x := 1
    return &x
}
```

If a value escapes the function, the runtime/compiler must ensure it outlives the stack frame.

q2.question: Why can returning `&x` be safe in Go?
options: Because pointers to locals are always invalid but tolerated.
Because escape analysis can move `x` to the heap when the value must outlive the function frame.
Because Go copies the stack frame forever.
explanations: Go does not knowingly expose invalid local pointers in safe code.
Correct. The compiler/runtime cooperates to preserve lifetime safety.
Whole stack frames are not copied forever.
q2.answerReason: Escape analysis helps decide when stack allocation is insufficient for the required lifetime.

q3.question: What is the core question escape analysis answers?
options: Whether the program imports too many packages.
Whether the pointer type implements an interface.
Whether a value’s lifetime requires it to outlive the current stack frame.
explanations: Imports are unrelated.
Interface implementation is unrelated.
Correct. Lifetime and reachability beyond the frame are the key questions.
q3.answerReason: Allocation placement follows lifetime needs, not just syntax.

16_gc_basic.title: Garbage Collection from Reachability
16_gc_basic.description:
Go uses garbage collection to reclaim heap objects that are no longer reachable from live roots.

```text
roots -> reachable objects stay alive
unreachable objects can be reclaimed
```

This lets developers avoid manual free operations, but runtime cost and allocation behavior still matter.

q2.question: What keeps an object alive for the garbage collector?
options: It is still reachable from live roots such as stacks, globals, or runtime-managed references.
It has existed for fewer than 10 seconds.
It was allocated with a pointer type.
explanations: Correct. Reachability is the key property.
Object age alone is not the rule.
Pointer type syntax alone does not guarantee liveness.
q2.answerReason: GC is about reachability from live roots, not about manual “delete” calls.

q3.question: Why should a Go developer still care how GC works?
options: Because every Go program must call `free` manually.
Because allocation rate, object lifetime, and pointer-rich structures affect runtime and pause/throughput behavior.
Because GC eliminates all memory-related performance tradeoffs.
explanations: Go does not require manual `free`.
Correct. GC removes one class of bug, but runtime costs still depend on allocation patterns.
GC does not erase performance tradeoffs.
q3.answerReason: Knowing the GC model helps you reason about heap pressure, latency, and why certain designs allocate more than others.

17_http_handler.title: HTTP Handlers and Request Flow
17_http_handler.description:
A handler is a function that receives a response writer and a request.

```go
func handler(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request)
```

This is the entry point for many Go web services.

q2.question: What is a handler’s job conceptually?
options: To replace the Go runtime scheduler.
To implement garbage collection.
To receive a request, run application logic, and write a response.
explanations: Scheduling is unrelated.
GC is unrelated.
Correct. A handler is the boundary between HTTP traffic and your application logic.
q2.answerReason: Handlers are transport-layer entry points for incoming requests.

q3.question: Why is request flow design important even in a simple handler?
options: Because clear boundaries between parsing, business logic, and response writing keep services maintainable.
Because handlers should contain every database query directly.
Because request methods are ignored by `net/http`.
explanations: Correct. Good boundaries matter early, not only in large services.
Pushing everything into the handler creates tight coupling.
HTTP method handling absolutely matters.
q3.answerReason: Even small services benefit when transport concerns and domain logic are kept distinct.
